---
title: "Day 6 — Concepts"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

Companion to the day-6 slides. A whirlwind tour of the pieces that make day 6's pure-Rust web stack work. Read this once before exercise 1; come back to specific sections as the day unfolds.

## WebAssembly in one paragraph

WebAssembly (WASM) is a compact, sandboxed bytecode format that runs in every modern browser. Rust compiles to WASM the same way it compiles to native machine code — pick a target (`wasm32-unknown-unknown`) and `cargo build`. The resulting `.wasm` blob loads from a `<script>` tag in your HTML and runs in the page with about the same performance as native code, no JavaScript runtime cost.

## The toolchain

```bash
rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown      # one-off: add the WASM target
cargo install trunk                            # one-off: install trunk
```

[Trunk](https://trunkrs.dev) is a build-and-serve tool that:

1. Compiles the frontend to WASM (`cargo build --target wasm32-unknown-unknown`).
2. Runs [`wasm-bindgen`](https://github.com/rustwasm/wasm-bindgen) to generate a thin JavaScript shim.
3. Bundles WASM + JS + your `index.html` into a static `dist/` directory.
4. Serves it over HTTP with hot reload on save.

## Workspaces

A workspace is one Cargo project containing several crates:

```toml
# Cargo.toml at the workspace root
[workspace]
resolver = "2"
members = ["shared", "backend", "frontend"]

[workspace.dependencies]
shared = { path = "shared" }
serde  = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
```

Each member has its own `Cargo.toml` and `src/`. Common dependencies declared once in `[workspace.dependencies]` are referenced by members with `serde.workspace = true`. One `target/` at the workspace root is shared across crates.

→ [Cargo Book: Workspaces](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/workspaces.html)

## The shared-types pattern

The `shared` crate holds types serialised over the wire:

```rust
// shared/src/lib.rs
use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct SampleRecord {
    pub id: String,
    pub name: String,
    pub sequence: String,
}
```

Both `backend` and `frontend` depend on `shared`. The frontend compiles `shared` to WASM; the backend compiles it natively. Because the *same* struct definition is used on both sides, the JSON wire format **cannot drift** — change a field name and both crates fail to compile until they agree again.

This is the single most important architectural idea of day 6.

## `serde`

```rust
#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]
struct Sample { id: String, name: String }
```

[`serde`](https://serde.rs/) is a serialisation framework — `Serialize` and `Deserialize` are traits the `derive` macro implements for you. [`serde_json`](https://docs.rs/serde_json/) is the JSON adapter:

```rust
let json: String   = serde_json::to_string(&sample)?;
let sample: Sample = serde_json::from_str(&json)?;
```

Most crates that handle JSON (`axum`, `gloo_net`, …) call `serde` for you.

## `axum` — Rust HTTP server

```rust
use axum::{routing::get, Json, Router};

async fn list_samples() -> Json<Vec<SampleSummary>> { /* ... */ }

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() -> std::io::Result<()> {
    let app = Router::new().route("/api/samples", get(list_samples));
    let listener = tokio::net::TcpListener::bind("127.0.0.1:3000").await?;
    axum::serve(listener, app).await
}
```

[`axum`](https://docs.rs/axum/) routes HTTP requests to async functions. [`Json<T>`](https://docs.rs/axum/latest/axum/struct.Json.html) does serde encoding automatically. Path parameters appear as [`axum::extract::Path<T>`](https://docs.rs/axum/latest/axum/extract/struct.Path.html) arguments.

## `async`/`await` — the minimum you need today

An `async fn` returns a **future** — a description of work to do, not the result. The future only runs when *awaited*. Inside an `async` block, `.await` yields control until the awaited future is ready:

```rust
async fn fetch_sample() -> Result<SampleRecord, gloo_net::Error> {
    let resp = Request::get("/api/samples/lambda").send().await?;
    let data = resp.json::<SampleRecord>().await?;
    Ok(data)
}
```

In WASM (the browser), [`wasm_bindgen_futures::spawn_local`](https://docs.rs/wasm-bindgen-futures/latest/wasm_bindgen_futures/fn.spawn_local.html) runs an async block on the browser's event loop:

```rust
wasm_bindgen_futures::spawn_local(async move {
    let r = fetch_sample().await;
    // ...
});
```

In axum (the server), the `#[tokio::main]` macro starts the [Tokio](https://tokio.rs) async runtime, which schedules futures across a thread pool.

For day 6 you do not need to understand `Future` internals or runtime mechanics — treat `.await` as *"pause here until the work is done; let the runtime do other things in the meantime"*.

## Yew — Rust UI framework

Modelled on React. Write components as functions, store state in hooks, return HTML from a macro.

### Function components

```rust
use yew::prelude::*;

#[function_component(App)]
fn app() -> Html {
    html! { <h1>{ "Hello" }</h1> }
}

fn main() {
    yew::Renderer::<App>::new().render();
}
```

The function re-runs on every render. Hooks make state survive across renders.

### Hooks

- [`use_state(initial)`](https://docs.rs/yew/0.23.0/yew/functional/fn.use_state.html) — state that persists across renders. Returns a handle you dereference to read (`*state`) and call `.set(new)` on to write.
- [`use_effect_with(deps, body)`](https://docs.rs/yew/0.23.0/yew/functional/fn.use_effect_with.html) — run a side effect when deps change. `deps = ()` means "once on mount".

### Callbacks

```rust
let on_click = Callback::from(move |_event| {
    // runs on click
});

html! { <button onclick={on_click}>{ "Click me" }</button> }
```

[`Callback::from`](https://docs.rs/yew/0.23.0/yew/callback/struct.Callback.html) wraps a closure so Yew can attach it to a DOM event. Clone before moving into nested closures.

### The `html!` macro

JSX-like syntax compiled into Rust at macro-expansion time:

- Every tag must be closed (`<br/>`, not `<br>`).
- Text content goes inside `{ }`: `<p>{ "hi" }</p>`.
- Any expression inside `{ }`: `<p>{ 1 + 1 }</p>` renders "2".
- `{ for iter }` renders an iterator of `Html` nodes.
- SVG elements work alongside HTML.

→ [Yew docs](https://yew.rs/)

## `gloo_net` — fetch from WASM

The WASM-side HTTP client:

```rust
use gloo_net::http::Request;

let resp = Request::get("/api/samples").send().await?;
let data: Vec<SampleSummary> = resp.json().await?;
```

Two `await`s: one for the response headers, one for the body. [`gloo_net`](https://docs.rs/gloo-net/) wraps the browser's native `fetch()` API.

## The Trunk dev proxy

When trunk serves the frontend on `:8080` and axum runs on `:3000`, the frontend's `fetch('/api/...')` would go to trunk, not the backend. The fix is one block in `Trunk.toml`:

```toml
[[proxy]]
rewrite = "/api/"
backend = "http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/"
```

Trunk forwards anything matching `/api/...` to axum. The frontend stays same-origin and CORS never comes up.

## SVG in `html!`

Yew's `html!` macro accepts SVG elements alongside HTML ones:

```rust
html! {
    <svg width="600" height="80" viewBox="0 0 600 80">
        <polyline points="0,40 100,30 200,50" fill="none" stroke="#2266aa" />
    </svg>
}
```

SVG uses screen-style coordinates (Y=0 at the top). Numeric attributes must be strings — `width={n.to_string()}` if `n` is a `u32`.

## What we deliberately leave out

The day-6 scope:

- **In**: function components + `use_state`/`use_effect_with`; `Callback::from`; one `async fn` + `spawn_local`; `gloo_net::Request`; serde wire format; axum routes + handlers; Trunk + the dev proxy; inline SVG.
- **Out**: trait objects vs generics for `Future`, executors, pinning; routers with state extractors and middleware in axum; the full Yew agents/component-lifecycle/portals API; `wasm-bindgen` deep magic.

The thing you have built by the end of day 6 is structurally identical to a real Rust full-stack application. The "out" items are real, useful, and learnable — but they will not stop you from shipping.

## Next steps if you keep going

- [**Leptos**](https://leptos.dev) — newer Yew alternative based on fine-grained reactivity (signals instead of virtual DOM diffing). Smaller WASM bundles.
- [**Dioxus**](https://dioxuslabs.com) — same UI code targets web, desktop, mobile, all from Rust.
- [**Tauri**](https://tauri.app) — Rust backend + web frontend bundled as a native desktop app.
- [**sqlx**](https://docs.rs/sqlx/) / [**SeaORM**](https://www.sea-ql.org/SeaORM/) — type-safe SQL from Rust; pairs cleanly with axum.

Outside the course but worth knowing the names of.
